Scenario.title: Energy Sector Cyber Espionage Campaign C0027
Scenario.overview: Energy Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting energy sector infrastructure leverages AI-driven reconnaissance, firmware corruption, and credential theft to compromise operational systems and exfiltrate sensitive data.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Target Artifact (Tasks), Firmware, Internet-facing assets, Application Log, Windows Registry, Network Traffic, OS Process, Credential Store, File, Process
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Firmware Modification, Delivery Method, Application Log Content, Windows Registry Key Creation, Network Traffic Content, Process Injection, Keylogging, Reg.exe Utility, Pass the Hash, File Modification, Process Creation

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker employs AI algorithms to analyze publicly available data on energy sector infrastructure and employee schedules, identifying high-value targets and potential vulnerabilities in operational workflows.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC-682: Exploitation of Firmware or ROM Code with Unpatchable Vulnerabilities
stage.description: The threat actor develops custom malware payloads using open-source frameworks like Metasploit, embedding them in firmware to bypass traditional security controls and establish persistent footholds.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2016-9079 (Suggested): Mozilla Firefox, Firefox ESR, and Thunderbird contain a use-after-free vulnerability in SVG Animation
stage.description: The attacker deploys spear-phishing emails with malicious Word/Excel attachments containing embedded macros, exploiting CVE-2016-9079 to execute payload delivery and establish initial command-and-control communication.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2017-0148 (Suggested): The SMBv1 server in Microsoft allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via crafted packets
stage.description: The attacker triggers malicious code execution via compromised Office documents, leveraging CVE-2017-0148 to exploit SMBv1 vulnerabilities and deploy the initial payload across the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attacker creates scheduled tasks in the Windows Registry to maintain persistent access, ensuring continued operation of the malware even after system reboots or remediation attempts.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: The attacker scans internal network traffic patterns to identify unpatched systems and exploit known elevation-of-privilege vulnerabilities, gaining administrative access to critical infrastructure.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: The attacker injects malicious code into legitimate system processes to evade detection by security software while maintaining covert control over compromised endpoints.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: A keylogger is deployed to capture login credentials during user sessions, enabling the attacker to extract usernames and passwords for further lateral movement and data exfiltration.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: The attacker uses the Reg.exe utility to monitor network traffic and map internal network architecture, identifying critical assets and potential pathways for data exfiltration.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: The attacker moves laterally across the network using stolen credentials via the "Pass the Hash" technique, accessing additional systems without requiring direct authentication.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: The attacker harvests sensitive operational data from servers, including SCADA configurations and maintenance logs, before corrupting files to erase forensic evidence of the breach.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Data is exfiltrated through encrypted tunnels created by malicious processes, masking the traffic as normal network activity to avoid detection by perimeter defenses.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: The final stage involves corrupting industrial control system files to trigger operational disruptions, such as power grid outages or equipment malfunctions, achieving strategic impact on energy infrastructure.

Scenario.summary: The C0027 campaign demonstrates a coordinated attack lifecycle targeting energy sector infrastructure, combining low-skill phishing with advanced firmware exploitation and data destruction to achieve both espionage and operational disruption.
